nodes.name: Sherman Robertson
nodes.summary: Sherman Robertson was a blues singer, guitarist and songwriter.

nodes.name: Shirley Griffith
nodes.summary: Shirley Griffith was a blues singer, songwriter and guitarist.

nodes.name: Sippie Wallace
nodes.summary: Texas-born singer, pianist and songwriter whose classic-blues recordings and later revival connected the 1920s recording era to new audiences.

nodes.name: Sista Monica Parker
nodes.summary: Sista Monica Parker was a blues songwriter.

nodes.name: Sister Rosetta Tharpe
nodes.summary: Arkansas-born singer-guitarist who brought amplified electric guitar, rhythmic drive and gospel repertoire into popular performance decades before rock and roll's rise.

nodes.name: Skip James
nodes.summary: Developed an eerie minor-key, open-tuning style distinct from the Patton lineage; rediscovered in the 1960s and covered by Cream.

nodes.name: Smokey Hogg
nodes.summary: Smokey Hogg was a blues musician and songwriter.

nodes.name: Smoky Babe
nodes.summary: Smoky Babe was a blues singer, composer and musician.

nodes.name: Snooks Eaglin
nodes.summary: Snooks Eaglin was a blues guitarist, jazz musician and jazz guitarist.

nodes.name: Son House
nodes.summary: A fierce slide guitarist and preacher whose intensity directly mentored Robert Johnson and later re-emerged as a touchstone for the 1960s blues revival.

nodes.name: Son Seals
nodes.summary: Son Seals was a blues singer, guitarist and composer.

nodes.name: Sonny Boy Williamson II
nodes.summary: Toured Britain in the 1960s backed by the Yardbirds and Animals, giving young British musicians direct stage experience with a first-generation bluesman.

nodes.name: Sonny Rhodes
nodes.summary: Sonny Rhodes was a blues musician.

nodes.name: Sonny Rodgers
nodes.summary: Sonny Rodgers was a blues singer.

nodes.name: Sonny Terry
nodes.summary: Georgia-born singer and harmonica player whose expressive whoops, train effects and rhythmic playing became a defining Piedmont blues sound.

nodes.name: Speckled Red
nodes.summary: Speckled Red was a blues singer, jazz musician and pianist.

nodes.name: Negro Spirituals
nodes.summary: Religious songs built on call-and-response and coded imagery, sung in churches and fields alike; the shared melodic and emotional vocabulary of spirituals and blues led some scholars to call them sacred and secular siblings.

nodes.name: St. Louis Jimmy Oden
nodes.summary: St. Louis Jimmy Oden was a blues composer, singer and songwriter.

nodes.name: Stevie Ray Vaughan
nodes.summary: Rebuilt mainstream interest in blues guitar in the 1980s by synthesizing Albert King, Hendrix, and Texas blues into a ferocious modern style.

nodes.name: Susan Tedeschi
nodes.summary: Susan Tedeschi is a blues singer-songwriter, singer and composer.

nodes.name: Sylvester Weaver
nodes.summary: Sylvester Weaver was a blues musician, musician and guitarist.

nodes.name: T-Bone Walker
nodes.summary: Pioneered electric lead-guitar blues showmanship in the 1940s, the direct template B.B. King cited for his own style.

nodes.name: Tab Benoit
nodes.summary: Tab Benoit is a blues musician and guitarist.

nodes.name: Tail Dragger Jones
nodes.summary: Tail Dragger Jones was a blues singer.

nodes.name: Taj Mahal
nodes.summary: Singer, guitarist and multi-instrumentalist whose career connects country blues with Caribbean, African, Hawaiian and other roots-music traditions.

nodes.name: Tampa Red
nodes.summary: Tampa Red was a blues musician, pianist and guitarist.

nodes.name: Ted Hawkins
nodes.summary: Ted Hawkins was a blues musician and singer-songwriter.

nodes.name: Teddy Darby
nodes.summary: Teddy Darby was a blues musician, singer and guitarist.

nodes.name: Teeny Tucker
nodes.summary: Teeny Tucker is a blues songwriter and musician.

nodes.name: Terry Garland
nodes.summary: Terry Garland was a blues singer and songwriter.

nodes.name: Terry "Harmonica" Bean
nodes.summary: Terry "Harmonica" Bean is a blues songwriter.

nodes.name: Thornetta Davis
nodes.summary: Thornetta Davis is a blues singer and musician.

nodes.name: Tinsley Ellis
nodes.summary: Tinsley Ellis is a blues musician, guitarist and composer.

nodes.name: Titus Turner
nodes.summary: Titus Turner was a blues singer and songwriter.

nodes.name: Tommy Johnson
nodes.summary: Known for a falsetto break and the 'Crossroads' myth later attached to Robert Johnson; influential on the North Mississippi hill-country sound.

nodes.name: Travis Leonard Blaylock
nodes.summary: Travis Leonard Blaylock was a blues singer.

nodes.name: U.P. Wilson
nodes.summary: U.P. Wilson was a blues singer, songwriter and musician.

nodes.name: Victoria Spivey
nodes.summary: Texas singer, pianist, songwriter, bandleader and label owner whose work bridged classic blues recording, postwar performance and the blues revival.

nodes.name: Walter Roland
nodes.summary: Walter Roland was a blues singer, jazz musician and pianist.

nodes.name: Willie "Big Eyes" Smith
nodes.summary: American electric blues vocalist, harmonica player, drummer, bandleader, and composer associated with Chicago blues.

nodes.name: Willie Brown
nodes.summary: A close musical partner of Patton and House, tying together the Dockery-area Delta scene through shared performances and recordings.

nodes.name: Willie Cobbs
nodes.summary: Willie Cobbs was a blues musician and singer.

nodes.name: Willie Dixon
nodes.summary: Chess Records' chief songwriter and bassist, penning standards recorded by Muddy Waters, Howlin' Wolf, and later Cream, Led Zeppelin, and the Rolling Stones.

nodes.name: Woodrow Adams
nodes.summary: Woodrow Adams was a blues musician.

nodes.name: Wynonie Harris
nodes.summary: Wynonie Harris was a blues singer, jazz musician and guitarist.

nodes.name: The Yardbirds
nodes.summary: A proving ground for Clapton, Beck, and Page, translating Chicago and Delta blues into extended, amplified 'rave-up' improvisation.

nodes.name: Z. Z. Hill
nodes.summary: Z. Z. Hill was a blues singer and musician.
